speaker: interviewer
text: We'll keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Every minute of our life was something which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Breder, please continue your march from Dresden to your home, to Prague, and then to your home. We'd like to hear about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dresden was a city completely leveled. So we walked around Dresden. Here and there, we were able to-- to get some food. We were afraid to go to houses, which were still standing ask for it. But it was May, also. And the cherries started to ripen outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naturally, where we see they're not completely ripe. We were helping ourselves with that. Here and there, we were able, from the Russian armies, to get some food. And some of that we were able to steal. We had enough practice in camp. Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sleeped-- slept always outside somewhere. We were afraid to go to a German house. And we were afraid to ask the Russian army, because, you know, soldiers after the war. Many were, you know, raped. But we didn't look like raping-- we didn't look too good, so someone could rape us. And we were lucky, too. So what we slept on, we had-- we carried along blankets, light blankets, each one of us right after we were liberated. So we got some clothing from the Russian army, the Liberation Army. And some of them we stole when we were in this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we prepared ourselves. We won't sleep in any house, because we were frightened. Survive, you know, so long in a camp, and then being killed just here, it was not our goal. So we survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dresden is very close to the Czech border, very close, so maybe two, three day's walk. And then we passed the border. So we got food from the Czech families no problem. They were very, very helpful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then-- then we came finally to Prague. In Prague, we went also to-- I think it was they had set up for refugees some centers. So we were there. And we were there then. They housed us very nice in some castle. When you say they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Czechs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech government, or people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government, yes. The government agencies. And we spent, then, until June, or maybe July, in Prague. Then, you know, it was time to go. And I wanted to go and see if someone from my family besides my immediate family survived. Because in Auschwitz, I knew already from later transports who came later than we, the first, that families were taken two months later in 1942. And from my city, I met, you know, who were selected for life. I met. And they said my whole family went in June in the transport, but she knows went to Auschwitz. And I knew-- I was already in Canada-- that nobody survived from that. So I know my three brothers, my sister, everybody was wiped out. But anyway, I wanted to go home. I said, [INAUDIBLE] maybe somebody else survived. So from Prague, only once a week went a train to Bratislava. So I went with the rest of the girls. We separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody wanted to go to a different part, because we were not from the same city, the girls with whom I survived, but close by. So finally, we found a train. And the only room-- because it was loaded with refugees-- was on the top of the train. But I was so young. Why not on the top of the train? So I traveled back to Bratislava. It took us I don't know how many days. It took us I don't know how many days we came to Bratislava. In Bratislava, when we came-- Excuse me, we? Who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls, I don't know, two or three who wanted to go the same road like I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you hadn't separated yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of us. Not all of us. Because many of them were staying still in Prague. They didn't want to go home. And Bratislava, we didn't have where to go. So the joint committee was already there. So we got a little-- like, like-- how you say it-- to a hotel, we all four. I remember, not far away from the rail station. We were I don't know how many in one room, because there were so many refugees. And the other day, two young men came and searched [BACKGROUND NOISE] for relatives, too. And I looked up, and those were my two cousins. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One of them died in Los Angeles a couple of years ago. And I looked at them. And they were asking, did you see so and so? Did you see so and so? Do you know where-- everybody was somewhere. Did you saw-- remember the name? And they found me. And so then, I left with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin had already a place in Bratislava. He was a goldsmith. And he has his jewelry store in Los Angeles there now. And then the other cousin said his mother is alive, [? Hovesteta ?]. And they survived in hiding in Slovakia and with partisans. And his mother, my mother's sister, my aunt, survived being a cook as a non-Jew in another part of Slovakia in a farmhouse. But we had to travel to the Presov to the east from Bratislava, because she was there. So then, we all three went up there. But my aunt, two of her daughters went with me the same time. And they didn't survive. And when I came there, she said, where is Libo, the same name like me, and [? Serena? ?] I had to tell her the story what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine how she felt and how I felt? But anyway, I was-- she has a nice house there in Presov. I stayed with her until-- I think so-- about the end of the year. And then I went to Bratislava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my cousin, like I said, he has already a workshop. And he had an apartment in Bratislava. So he was-- he's only one year older than me. So I was the cook there, because I was the oldest of the family-- I was 20-- to survive. And then, you know, then when the-- when the cousins found me, they said, my sister, who was raised by my aunt, she was living in Hungary. And she worked in Budapest. She survived in Budapest. She said, you sister survived. But that's another story. I don't know if we have time for it. My sister survived-- she was younger-- she is younger, two years-- in Hungary. When they started collecting in 1944 Jews in Hungary, she lived in Kosice. That's not far from Presov, another big city. So she went to Budapest. And she got Aryan papers. She survived as a Catholic. But when they started to collect in Hungary in '44, in May, the Jews, she escaped through the borders back to Slovakia. And she came to Bratislava. But someone told her that the chief of police-- she didn't have papers-- is the former judge from Presov, who used to play cards in Presov with our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she went to him. And he gave her false papers. And his wife was the prima ballerina in the theater in Bratislava. She was Bulgarian. And she-- they expected a baby, so he took her into his house as a nanny. And that, she survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, when they picked up me, she was still the nanny in his house. So they went and picked her up. And so then, you know, she went to school, back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it when you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met, you know, in my aunt's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were not raised together. We see-- saw each other only on vacation time. So, you know, we didn't have too much in common. But we were sisters. And we were-- we are very close now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she lives here in Daly City. And the rest, my aunt and whom I met, survived as partisans. And one cousin from the jewelry sister, she married also the head, you know, the head of the partisans, you know, big Communist. And she still lives in Bratislava. And how long did you stay in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1966. Then the marriage is another thing. You know, when we were in Bratislava-- I moved back to Bratislava from Presov to my cousin. And I was the cook. My sister went to school. And we went to the Office of the Joint Committee where we received every month a package of clothing, of food and some clothing. And we were staying in line and waiting until [? our ?] turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind me was, you know, young man. And I turned around. I said, where did you survive? And he said, in Auschwitz. And I said, did you find someone? He said, no. I'm alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I found a cousin and an aunt. And then I invited him to come and see us. We had already an apartment. And I ask him what he does. He said, he's here. And he lives in a former girlfriend's house. She's married. And it was Fred.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1945. December, I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: January, '4-- he knows better. And we dated. And August 18, we married. And we lived in Bratislava until 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there professionally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- first of all, you know, I was kicked out when I was 14 from school. So I signed up for, you know-- I got my diploma. And then I started to study. You know, I had to take everything. It was a kind of a college, two years, but in the evenings. Because I was married already. But our son was born in 1948, two years after we married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul. Pavel. And I wanted-- because I needed very badly a job. Fritz, he was working in a bakery. His parents were bakers. So he saw what's going on and learned the trade at home. And the only thing that he could do was, you know, what he was familiar with. He got the job, night job, also. And he hated that. But hate or not hate, we needed to eat. And we lived [BACKGROUND NOISE] at my cousin's place in [INAUDIBLE]. And finally, he was able to get restitution back, the property, and the bakery from his parents' house. And he sold that. And then we could purchase with this money an apartment for ourselves. What we did, and bought furniture. And then, you know, like I said, I was still cooking for my sister and my cousin. And then, in 1948, you know, Czechoslovakia, after the war, got back to democracy. In 1948, you know, everything changed again. The Communists took over and won the elections and became a satellite of Russia, like all the countries. Ours was in '48. But one thing which I forgot to tell you. I have to mention. The welcome when I came back to my parents' house where we lived for many, many years, the [NON-ENGLISH] it means the house was sold for $1 to the [INAUDIBLE] the Nazis, the Slovakian Nazis. And when I came back I knocked-- to Stropkov-- I knocked on the door. It was not a door. It was a gate. You know, those houses in small cities, whole wooden, big gate. I knocked on the gate, went to go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: A guy came out and asked me, what do you want? And I said, well, I want go back to my house. He looked at me, slammed the door. And he said, go back where you came from. That was my welcome. So then I said-- I left, and never returned there. To Bratislava, back. And I said, we won't stay here anymore, because nobody wants us. We applied, when we get-- we got married for an American visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred had his family, his mother's sisters, who came to the United States in the 1900s. He had nine, or eight aunts. We-- we registered in 1946. In 1948, we had to wait for the quota number. In 1948, we still didn't get. You know, we didn't got the turn. We couldn't get the visa. When the Communists took over the country, they closed the borders. And we couldn't leave. We were trapped for 20 years in Czechoslovakia. We applied to leave the country year by year, always denied. But when I applied for a job in 1951-- after my second child was born, we couldn't live from one salary-- they didn't take me. First I went, because I had already my education at [INAUDIBLE] what they ask for being able to work in a bookstore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the manager said, oh, we need [INAUDIBLE] So well, yes, you can come tomorrow, because we need badly somebody here. So I said, fine. But here is, he said, a questionnaire.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to fill it out. I filled out. I filled out where I was during war, what my religion is, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he looked at the application, he said, we don't need no more. Because he didn't want me, because I was a Jewish. So what did I do? Next time, I went for application, I didn't fill out the column my religion. I didn't fill out where I was during the war. I was accepted. In the same store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same store?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same store. And I worked there for 15 years until we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed in 1966 that made it possible for you to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Communists was softer. Dubcek was there already. And, you know, it was, you know, you know, somehow, the [INAUDIBLE], the younger generation didn't want to be so, you know, I mean, one sided, I would say. They were more open. They had the chance more to get to the West, to read. So it was, like he said, they wanted Communism with a human face.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I just go back a little, since you lived there 20 years and were trapped. What was it like for you, as a Jewish person, to worship in Czechoslovakia at that time during the last 20 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't worship at all. We didn't have a Jewish community there. What you talking? We didn't have a rabbi. We didn't have kosher. We didn't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who'd returned? And who returned left the country. Many legal-- I never wanted illegal. Many left and were-- were shot on the borders. Surviving three years, and then risk my life? Somehow, like in [? Lager, ?] I always said, maybe it will change, you know? We'll get an exit visa. Was there anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] [? Fred ?] will tell you that story, how he was the only person on the [INAUDIBLE] for the [INAUDIBLE]. They kicked him out, only Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you raise your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, then, a big trail with [INAUDIBLE] like in Russia, was the trial in Hungary, was the trial-- the accused them, innocent, and tried them, and murdered them, only because they were Jews. It went on all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you raised your children, how did you raise them, as far as Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't raise them. I didn't talk about that. Because they had nowhere to go, [? let's see, ?] and get, you know, education. It was no-- no-- I mean, it was no good for me, as a mother, for us, us parents, to teach them of that and then be hated by the [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway they pointed at them, they are Jewish. Because the neighborhood knew we are Jews. But we didn't have a chance to be a community, to educate our children. And we didn't have belonging to anywhere, to a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other Jewish families were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, then, after 1966?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1966 We were already in America. 1944-- all the 20 years, we were just asking exit visa. Because we got already a permit from the United-- from the embassy. We got the affidavit. We could go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Slovaks wouldn't let us go. They didn't want us. And they didn't want to let us go. [INAUDIBLE] But finally, we got a permit, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left. In two weeks, we were in Vienna. And we spent two months in Vienna waiting for the rest of the American permits. And we came March 31st to America. '66.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you bring with you as far as objects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four children and two suitcases-- two children and four suitcases. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What precious items did you have in your suitcases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything that we accumulated in 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special objects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, $40. [LAUGHS] Now, I'm laughing. It's not a laughing matter. To start in America is very hard. When I recall, the first days, no language. Nothing. It was bitter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was here to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was here already. My sister, you know, I told you, she was hiding in Hungary. But she was a surgical nurse. My brother-in-law is also a Hungarian native of Budapest. He played soccer, good soccer, an international soccer player for Iskra Bratislava. That was a club. And he paid for [INAUDIBLE] in Vienna before. But the club bought him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened? He played against the club in Prague, [INAUDIBLE]. And they broke his leg. But he wanted to go. But his club was from Bratislava, so brought him back. But they brought him-- it still was operating, a Jewish hospital in Bratislava. And my sister was there the nurse. And she put his broken ankle here somewhere in a [NON-ENGLISH]. How you say? Cast.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a cast. And, you know, they got to know each other. But in 1949, FC Milano bought him. So he went from Bratislava with the club to FC Milano. He got $20,000 just to join the club. And my sister, the last minute in '49, when the Russians-- you know, the Russian-- the Czechs, you know, couldn't go. You couldn't move. You couldn't go out. His cousin-- Fred's cousin-- his, my husband's, Fred's cousin, was the manager of the Prague airport. And he called him. He said, my sister-in-law has to leave. Because otherwise, she's going to marry Gene, who is in Milan. And she meets a ticket. She had already the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he arranged the ticket on the airplane from Prague to Milan. And so, she left. And they married in Milan. And their [? first ?] daughter was born in Milan. Then later, she had-- she has one daughter born in Milan, one son born in Belgium. Because he went from club to club. So just-- so she came here in 1953. Yes. Or later-- '54, '53. She came first to Chicago, because he was playing in Cicero. It's 200,000 Czechs in the soccer club in Cicero. And he was already then 30. You know, 30 for a soccer player is old. So the Maccabi here in San Francisco wanted him. So he came to San Francisco. But the Maccabi didn't pay too much, only when they had a match. So they found him a job at the Hotel Fairmont. So Hotel Fairmont-- but he didn't have any idea what to do. But two young men, the [? Levine ?] brothers, were there. And they helped him with the language. And when Hilton was built, the asked him, Gene, you want to come with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are going to the Hilton. He said, yes. So he was then a manager of the banquets, [INAUDIBLE], my brother-in-law. And he was working 30 years for the Hilton. He retired two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about you, and your husband, and your two children came to San Francisco, then, in 1966. And without language, how did you become familiar?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came the 1st of April. And we had the case worker from the Jewish family service. We went to register, because they wouldn't take them back to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dasha, my daughter, was 14. Paul was 16. So they had to go back, yes? So we registered them. But since they couldn't go, only in September to school, we registered for English classes. So me and the two children went every day for four hours for English preparing the children for the school. I went with them. Fred found a job two weeks later when we came in the bakery, the Ukraine bakery. So we [? bread ?] to eat. The Jewish family service paid the first month for the apartment. So we had a roof. And we got, I think, $200 for furniture. So we bought mattresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we slept on the floor. We had the room. And he brought bread. We didn't have to buy from the bakery. And from the salary what he made, it covered the expenses for the rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened? Dasha-- you know, our English, by September, eh. You know. She was thrown back one year in school [? in ?] September. And Paul went to school, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had to sign-- the-- when the-- it was, this time, draft, a draft. When they call him, he has to go. He went.
